_meta.authoringNotes: Classification by Doha agreement on groin pain in athletes (Weir et al. BJSM 2015, PMID 26031643). Adductor longus injury epidemiology per Werner et al. BJSM 2009 UEFA injury study (PMID 19460763). Return-to-play data per Serner et al. BJSM 2020 (PMID 31959676). Conservative vs surgical adductor outcome data per Schilders et al. AJSM 2009 (PMID 19193609).

h1Title: Groin Pull: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A groin pull is an acute strain or tear of one of the adductor muscles of the inner thigh, most often the adductor longus near its bony origin on the pubis. Among male professional athletes in sprinting, cutting, and kicking sports it accounts for 10-18% of all time-loss injuries (UEFA Elite Club Injury Study, Werner 2009). The typical mechanism is a sudden change of direction, a forceful kick, or a slip with the leg abducted; pain at the inner thigh near the pubis is immediate. Grade 1 strains recover in 1-2 weeks, grade 3 complete tears can keep athletes out for 8-12 weeks or longer. Early rest, ice, graded resistance work led by the Copenhagen adduction protocol, and progressive return-to-sport criteria deliver full recovery in over 90% of cases without surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 10-18% of male elite football time-loss injuries are groin-related (Werner et al. BJSM 2009); 4-6% in elite hockey, sprint, and rugby cohorts
keyStats.demographics: Men injured 4-5x more often than women in cutting sports; peak in athletes aged 18-30
keyStats.avgAge: Most acute groin pulls occur between ages 18 and 35 in competitive athletes; recreational injuries spread more evenly across adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1.0-1.5 million sport-related adductor strain injuries per year worldwide (extrapolated from sports injury registries)

definition: A groin pull (ICD-10: S76.211 unilateral adductor strain; S76.219 unspecified) is an acute injury of one of the adductor muscles or tendons of the medial thigh — most commonly adductor longus, less often adductor brevis, adductor magnus, gracilis, or pectineus — producing pain at the muscle-tendon junction or proximal enthesis on the pubic bone. The injury results from rapid eccentric loading during change-of-direction running, kicking, sliding tackles, or sudden splits when the leg is forcibly abducted and externally rotated against an adducting contraction. The Doha agreement on groin pain in athletes (2015) classifies the broader groin-pain spectrum into adductor-related, iliopsoas-related, inguinal-related, pubic-related, and hip-related categories, recognizing that acute strains overlap clinically with chronic tendinopathy and pubic-bone stress. The injury is graded by tissue disruption: grade 1 (microscopic damage, no architectural change, pain on resisted adduction), grade 2 (partial tear with discontinuity but intact tendon), and grade 3 (complete rupture or avulsion from the pubic insertion). Acute injuries are managed by sports medicine physicians, primary care doctors, and physiotherapists; chronic adductor-related groin pain frequently warrants imaging and a specialist sports clinic referral.

typesClassification.description: Microscopic muscle fiber damage with pain on resisted adduction but full active range of motion and minimal weakness. Local tenderness at the muscle-tendon junction. Most athletes return to sport within 1-2 weeks.

Partial tear with palpable defect or hematoma, definite weakness on resisted adduction (often 30-50% loss), and an antalgic gait. Return to sport typically takes 3-6 weeks with structured rehabilitation.

Complete tendon disruption, often at the proximal enthesis on the inferior pubic ramus. Athletes describe a 'pop' and immediate inability to bear weight. Recovery 8-12 weeks; selected complete avulsions may be considered for surgical repair.

Insertional degenerative change at the adductor longus origin from repetitive overload. Pain on activity, morning stiffness, focal tenderness on the pubis. Often follows incomplete rehabilitation of acute strain. Responds slowly to eccentric loading.

A traction injury at the secondary ossification center of the pubis in skeletally immature athletes, producing a bony avulsion rather than soft-tissue tear. Most heal conservatively; displaced fragments more than 2 cm may need fixation.

primarySymptoms: Sudden sharp pain at the inner thigh near the groin during a sprint, cut, kick, or slip — often described as a sudden grab or pop.
Localized tenderness on palpation along the adductor longus muscle or at its insertion on the pubic bone.
Pain reproduced by resisted hip adduction (squeezing the knees together against an examiner's hands or a ball).
Pain on passive hip abduction, especially with the hip extended.
Bruising along the inner thigh appearing 24-72 hours after the injury in grade 2 and grade 3 strains.
Antalgic gait with the affected leg externally rotated to offload the adductor.
Weakness or inability to lift the leg laterally while lying on the side.
Swelling and a palpable defect in the muscle belly in moderate to severe tears.
Difficulty climbing stairs, putting on socks, or stepping into a car.
Persistent dull ache after activity that fails to settle within 2 weeks (suggests ongoing low-grade tear or tendinopathy).
earlyWarningSigns: Tightness or pulling sensation in the inner thigh during the warm-up but no acute injury
Mild discomfort after intense sprinting or kicking that resolves the next day
Reduced single-leg squat depth or hip range of motion on the affected side
Lower adductor squeeze strength than usual on a routine pre-season screen
Recurrent low-grade groin soreness in the days after sport
emergencySigns: Sudden severe pain with an audible 'pop' and complete inability to bear weight — possible grade 3 tear or avulsion fracture
Visible large hematoma, deformity, or muscle defect in the inner thigh
Numbness or tingling radiating into the inner thigh or scrotum — possible obturator nerve involvement
Severe one-sided scrotal or labial pain with marked swelling — exclude testicular torsion or strangulated hernia
Fever with localized groin tenderness — exclude septic adductor abscess or osteomyelitis of the pubis

causes.cause: Rapid change of direction or cutting maneuver
causes.description: When the athlete plants the foot and pivots the opposite way, the adductor longus contracts eccentrically while the hip is forcibly abducted. Peak forces at the proximal tendon exceed tensile capacity and fibers tear. Football, hockey, basketball, rugby, and racquet sports all share this mechanism.

During a powerful kick the planted leg's adductors stabilize the pelvis while the kicking leg's adductors decelerate hip abduction. Mistimed kicks, kicks against opposing force (blocked shots), or sudden change in plant-leg position injure the muscle-tendon junction or pubic insertion.

causes.cause: Sudden splits or slip on a slippery surface
causes.description: Loss of footing on wet grass, ice, gym floor, or in a sliding tackle stretches the adductor beyond its working length under load. The injury occurs at end-range hip abduction and is more likely when the muscle is fatigued.

causes.cause: Inadequate warm-up and chronic muscle imbalance
causes.description: Cold muscle has reduced compliance and a lower elongation threshold. Athletes with weak adductors relative to abductors (adductor:abductor strength ratio under 80%) are at significantly higher risk in cohort studies.

A prior adductor strain doubles the risk of recurrence in the following season. Incomplete rehabilitation, persistent strength deficits, and residual scar tissue at the enthesis reduce the tissue's tolerance to load.

Cumulative microtrauma from running, kicking, and cutting in sports played year-round drives tendinous degeneration at the pubic insertion. Pain is gradual at first but a sudden acute episode often supervenes on chronic background tendinopathy.

riskFactors.factor: Sport involving cutting, kicking, sprinting, or skating
riskFactors.description: Football, ice hockey, rugby, Australian rules football, soccer, basketball, and racquet sports show the highest adductor strain incidence — up to 18% of time-loss injuries in elite male football per UEFA data.
Prior strain doubles the risk of recurrence over the next season because of residual strength deficit and altered tissue properties at the enthesis.
Pre-season squeeze tests showing adductor strength under 80% of abductor strength predict adductor injury risk increases of 2-3x. The Copenhagen adduction exercise corrects this imbalance.
Limited hip internal rotation (under 25°) is associated with adductor-related groin pain and femoroacetabular impingement; the latter may be a contributing morphology in chronic cases.
Men sustain adductor strains 4-5x more often than women in cutting sports, partly because of higher kicking and sprinting volumes and biomechanical pelvic-loading differences.
Cumulative tissue load and reduced tendon compliance contribute to higher injury rates in athletes over 25 within the same sport.

riskFactors.factor: Pre-season fitness gap or schedule congestion
riskFactors.description: Insufficient pre-season conditioning and fixture congestion during peak season correlate with elevated muscle strain rates in elite squads.

affectedDemographics: Elite male footballers: 10-18% of all time-loss injuries are groin-related (UEFA Elite Club Injury Study).
Ice hockey players: adductor strains account for approximately 10% of injuries; NHL goaltenders are particularly affected by chronic adductor tendinopathy.
Recreational athletes: account for the majority of overall cases by volume; most are mild and self-managed.
Adolescents and young adults aged 14-22 in cutting sports show increased risk of bony avulsion fractures rather than soft-tissue tears.
Female athletes have lower absolute incidence but rising rates as participation in football, hockey, and rugby grows.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is overwhelmingly clinical. The history confirms a single sudden mechanism — cutting, kicking, slipping, or stretching — and pain localized to the inner thigh. Examination follows a structured approach: inspection for bruising and defect; palpation of the adductor longus, adductor brevis, gracilis, pectineus, and the pubic insertions; passive hip range of motion; resisted hip adduction (the 'squeeze test' at 0°, 45°, and 90° hip flexion); single adductor stretch tests; and palpation of the conjoint tendon, inguinal canal, and pubic symphysis to exclude inguinal hernia, athletic pubalgia, and osteitis pubis. The Doha agreement framework guides which clinical entity is dominant — adductor-related, iliopsoas-related, inguinal-related, pubic-related, or hip-related groin pain — and these often coexist. Imaging is not required for grade 1 strains. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging in moderate to severe injuries and shows muscle fiber disruption, hematoma, and tendon avulsion in real time. MRI is reserved for grade 3 tears, persistent symptoms past 4-6 weeks, suspicion of avulsion fracture, and chronic tendinopathy — it grades fiber disruption, detects pubic bone marrow edema, and distinguishes osteitis pubis. Plain radiographs identify pediatric avulsion fractures and degenerative pubic symphyseal changes. Adductor squeeze strength at 0° and 45° measured with a hand-held dynamometer or sphygmomanometer correlates with severity and predicts return-to-sport timelines.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduces pain and quantifies strength deficit at the adductor-pubic complex
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies supine with hips flexed 0°, 45°, or 90°. The examiner places a fist or pressure cuff between the knees and asks the patient to squeeze. Reproduction of inner-thigh pain or measurable strength loss confirms adductor involvement. Test takes under 2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Localizes the injury to muscle-tendon junction versus pubic insertion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in supine position with hip slightly abducted. Examiner palpates the tendon from origin on the inferior pubic ramus distally. Focal tenderness at the bony origin suggests insertional injury or tendinopathy; tenderness 5-10 cm distal suggests muscle-tendon junction strain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms tear location, grades partial vs complete disruption, identifies hematoma, and guides any needle interventions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies supine with knee slightly flexed. The sonographer applies water-based gel and a high-frequency probe over the adductor. Real-time dynamic imaging during contraction adds information. 15-20 minutes; no ionizing radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the pelvis with adductor protocol
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive imaging for grade 3 tears, chronic groin pain, pubic bone marrow edema, athletic pubalgia, and pre-surgical planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies still in the scanner for 30-45 minutes. No injection unless an MR arthrogram is requested. Reports detail fiber disruption, hematoma volume, tendon retraction, pubic edema, and any concomitant rectus abdominis or inguinal canal pathology.

diagnosticTests.test: Hip range of motion and impingement testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coexisting femoroacetabular impingement or labral pathology that contributes to chronic adductor overload
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Passive hip internal and external rotation in 90° flexion (FADIR and FABER tests). Reduced internal rotation under 25° or pain in the anterior groin on FADIR raises suspicion of impingement and warrants pelvic AP and Dunn-view radiographs.

diagnosticTests.test: Inguinal hernia examination and cough impulse
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes inguinal or femoral hernia in the differential of acute or chronic groin pain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Examiner palpates the inguinal canal with the patient standing and asks for a cough; an expansile bulge supports hernia. A negative exam in a high-risk patient may warrant Valsalva ultrasound or herniography.

treatmentOverview: Acute management follows the POLICE principle (Protection, Optimal Loading, Ice, Compression, Elevation) — modern guidance has moved away from prolonged rest because complete immobilization weakens the muscle and delays return to sport. First 48-72 hours: ice 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours, compression shorts, crutches if antalgic gait is unavoidable, paracetamol or topical NSAIDs for pain; oral NSAIDs are used for short courses (3-5 days) but routine use in muscle injury remains debated because of theoretical impairment of satellite cell repair. From day 3-5 a structured progressive loading program begins under physiotherapy: isometric adduction at multiple hip angles, then concentric and eccentric strengthening (Copenhagen adduction protocol — randomized data show roughly 41% reduction in groin injuries when used as a prevention exercise; the same exercises drive rehabilitation), core and hip stabilization, sport-specific reintroduction. Return-to-sport criteria are functional, not time-based: pain-free squeeze test at 0° and 45° at greater than 90% of the contralateral side, full sprint without compensation, kicking or cutting drills without pain, and clinician clearance. Average return-to-play in elite cohorts: grade 1, 7-14 days; grade 2, 21-42 days; grade 3, 56-84 days. Refractory cases consider platelet-rich plasma (mixed evidence), corticosteroid injection at the enthesis (short-term symptom relief but no structural benefit), or surgery — tenotomy of the adductor longus or repair of complete avulsions — reserved for chronic disease over 6 months that has failed structured rehabilitation. Chronic adductor tendinopathy responds best to progressive heavy slow resistance training over 12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physiotherapy with Copenhagen adduction protocol
medicalTreatments.description: Progressive isometric, concentric, then eccentric loading of the adductors led by a physiotherapist over 4-12 weeks. The Copenhagen adduction exercise (side plank with adductor squeeze) is the cornerstone for both rehabilitation and prevention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence by approximately 40% in randomized controlled trials; return-to-sport within target timelines in over 85% of athletes

medicalTreatments.name: Relative rest with POLICE protocol (first 72 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Protection, optimal loading, ice (10-15 min every 2-3 hours), compression shorts, elevation when supine. Replaces older PRICE/RICE recommendations to emphasize early gentle loading once acute pain subsides.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces early swelling and pain; full rest beyond 72 hours delays recovery in muscle strain literature

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol or short-course oral NSAIDs (3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesia for moderate pain. Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily or naproxen 500 mg twice daily for limited durations. Topical diclofenac or ibuprofen reduces systemic exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful pain reduction in 60-70% of patients within 48-72 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection at the enthesis
medicalTreatments.description: Used in chronic adductor tendinopathy or refractory enthesopathy when 12 weeks of rehabilitation has failed. Limited to no more than two injections per site per year because of theoretical tendon weakening.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Short-term pain reduction in 60-70% at 6-8 weeks; no consistent long-term structural benefit

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Concentrated autologous platelets injected under ultrasound guidance. Theoretical mechanism is delivery of growth factors that accelerate tendon healing. Evidence in adductor tendinopathy is mixed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mixed; meta-analyses suggest modest benefit over placebo at 6 months in some tendinopathies but not all RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Shockwave therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Extracorporeal shockwave applied to the adductor enthesis weekly for 3-5 sessions. Reasonable second-line option for chronic adductor-related groin pain that has not responded to rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 55-65% at 6 months in small cohort studies

surgicalOptions.name: Adductor tenotomy (partial release)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical division of part or all of the adductor longus tendon at its pubic origin. Considered after 6 months of failed conservative management of chronic adductor-related groin pain in selected athletes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to previous level of sport in 70-85% of selected athletes (Schilders et al. 2009); recovery typically 8-12 weeks

surgicalOptions.name: Adductor avulsion repair
surgicalOptions.description: Open or arthroscopic-assisted reattachment of a completely avulsed proximal adductor tendon to the pubis with suture anchors. Reserved for displaced complete ruptures or younger athletes with high functional demand.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to sport in 80-90% in published series, but the evidence base remains small

surgicalOptions.name: Concomitant hip arthroscopy for femoroacetabular impingement
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when chronic adductor pain coexists with cam or pincer impingement contributing to repetitive adductor overload. Reshapes the femoral head-neck junction or trims acetabular rim.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Return to sport in 78-87% of athletes with combined adductor and FAI symptoms

alternativeTreatments.name: Manual therapy and soft-tissue mobilization
alternativeTreatments.description: Therapist-applied massage and trigger-point release adjunct to active rehabilitation. May reduce post-exercise soreness and improve range of motion in early recovery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture or dry needling
alternativeTreatments.description: Limited evidence shows modest short-term pain reduction in chronic adductor tendinopathy when used alongside exercise therapy, though most randomized studies remain small.

whySeeSpecialist: Sports medicine or musculoskeletal specialist referral is reasonable for any groin pain lasting longer than two weeks, recurrent strains, suspected grade 2 or 3 injury, chronic adductor tendinopathy, or coexisting hip impingement. Specialists confirm diagnosis with ultrasound or MRI, structure return-to-sport progression, and consider PRP, shockwave, or surgery in refractory cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a sports medicine physician or musculoskeletal radiologist with experience in athletic groin pain and pelvic ultrasound. Ideal centers offer same-day ultrasound, MR access, and physiotherapy familiar with the Copenhagen protocol and Doha framework. Surgical referral should go to a high-volume hip or sports surgery center if chronic disease persists past 6 months.
hospitalCriteria: MRI access with dedicated pelvic and adductor protocols
Sports physiotherapy team experienced in the Copenhagen adduction protocol
Image-guided injection service for chronic enthesopathy
Hip preservation or sports surgery team for refractory chronic disease
keyFacilities: Sports medicine clinics with same-day imaging
University and professional team training facilities
preventionStrategies: Perform the Copenhagen adduction exercise twice weekly during pre-season and once weekly in-season — randomized data show approximately 41% reduction in groin injuries.
Maintain an adductor-to-abductor strength ratio of at least 80% measured with hand-held dynamometry.
Warm up for at least 10 minutes with dynamic movements (high knees, lunges, leg swings) before sprinting or kicking drills.
Progress training load gradually with no more than a 10% weekly increase in volume or intensity.
Address residual deficits after any groin injury before returning to full competition — pain-free squeeze test at greater than 90% of the contralateral side is the minimum standard.
Use the FIFA 11+ injury-prevention warm-up; it reduces overall lower-limb injuries by 30-40% in cohort studies.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid sudden return to full-intensity sport before completing graded loading rehabilitation.
Wear compression shorts during sport for the first 4-6 weeks after returning to play; modest benefit on perceived support.
Schedule rest days each week to allow tissue recovery and avoid cumulative microtrauma.
Stretch hip flexors, adductors, and abductors after, not before, training; static stretches before sprinting reduce force output.
Track squeeze-test scores monthly during the season as an early-warning marker for recurrence.
Hydrate well and maintain adequate dietary protein (1.4-2.0 g/kg/day) during recovery from grade 2 or 3 strains.

recommended: Adequate protein (1.4-2.0 g/kg/day) for muscle repair, distributed across 4-5 meals
Anti-inflammatory whole foods (oily fish, vegetables, berries, nuts, olive oil)
Vitamin D and calcium-rich foods to support bone and muscle health, especially in athletes with limited sun exposure
Sufficient carbohydrate (3-5 g/kg/day) to support training and recovery
avoid: Excessive alcohol — impairs muscle repair and tendon collagen synthesis
Chronic energy deficit (LEA / RED-S), particularly in female athletes — predisposes to soft-tissue injury
Smoking and nicotine products — reduce microcirculation and slow tendon healing
Long-term high-dose NSAID use during the active healing phase — theoretical impairment of satellite cell repair

exerciseGuidelines: Days 0-3: relative rest with pain-free range of motion, gentle isometric adduction below pain threshold. Days 3-14: progressive isometric and concentric strengthening, stationary cycling, pool walking. Weeks 2-6: Copenhagen adduction exercise (build from short-lever to long-lever side plank with adductor squeeze), single-leg stability work, and graded running. Weeks 6+: change-of-direction and sport-specific drills only when squeeze-test strength exceeds 90% of the uninjured side and movement is pain-free.
dailyManagement: Ice the inner thigh for 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours during the first 48-72 hours.
Wear compression shorts during waking hours in the first week.
Perform the prescribed Copenhagen adduction progression on alternate days from day 5 onwards.
Track pain scores and squeeze test results at home using a journal or app.
Avoid loaded hip-flexion movements (heavy squats, kicking) until pain-free with daily activities.
Sleep with a pillow between the knees for the first week to reduce overnight tension on the adductors.
prognosis: Recovery is excellent for most groin pulls with structured care. Grade 1 strains: full return to sport in 7-14 days, recurrence rate roughly 5-10% in elite cohorts. Grade 2: 21-42 days, recurrence 15-20% if rehabilitation is shortened. Grade 3 complete tears: 56-84 days with conservative management, longer with surgery, recurrence 10-15% with completed rehabilitation. Chronic adductor tendinopathy is the main long-term complication; up to 30% of athletes with poorly rehabilitated acute strains develop chronic groin pain that limits sport participation. Predictors of poor outcome are prior groin injury, persistent strength deficit at return-to-sport, coexisting femoroacetabular impingement, late initiation of structured rehabilitation, and a high-volume cutting or kicking sport at elite level. Surgery for chronic disease delivers return to sport in 70-90% of selected athletes. Long-term hip osteoarthritis risk is increased modestly in athletes with chronic adductor-related groin pain and concurrent FAI.
recoveryTimeline: Grade 1: pain settles within 5-10 days; return to running by day 10-14. Grade 2: pain resolves over 2-3 weeks; full sprint and cutting drills typically by week 4-6. Grade 3: walking without pain by 2-3 weeks; running by 6-8 weeks; full sport at 10-12 weeks. Chronic adductor tendinopathy: meaningful improvement over 8-12 weeks of heavy slow resistance work, with most athletes returning to sport at 12-16 weeks.
complications: Recurrent strain — single biggest risk; mitigated by completing the full rehabilitation program and meeting strength-based return-to-sport criteria.
Chronic adductor tendinopathy and persistent groin pain affecting up to 30% of inadequately rehabilitated acute strains.
Compensatory injury elsewhere (hamstring, hip flexor, lumbar spine) when athletes alter movement patterns to protect the groin.
Osteitis pubis or pubic bone marrow edema in chronic adductor overload — recognized on MRI; responds to load management and physiotherapy.
Femoroacetabular impingement contribution to recurrence — warrants imaging in chronic cases and possible hip arthroscopy.
Surgical complications (hematoma, infection, nerve injury) in fewer than 5% of adductor tenotomy or repair procedures.

supportResources.name: British Journal of Sports Medicine — groin pain in athletes
supportResources.description: Open-access summaries of the Doha agreement on groin pain and major adductor rehabilitation studies.

supportResources.name: FIFA Medical Network — 11+ injury prevention programme
supportResources.description: Free FIFA 11+ exercise programme with video instruction, used worldwide in football and other cutting sports.

supportResources.name: Aspetar Sports Medicine Hospital — groin pain resources
supportResources.description: Patient and clinician resources from the host of the Doha agreement consensus meeting on groin pain in athletes.

supportResources.name: American Medical Society for Sports Medicine (AMSSM)
supportResources.description: US sports medicine professional society offering athlete-facing condition pages and find-a-physician tools.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hamstring strains cause pain at the back of the thigh during sprinting, with tenderness over biceps femoris, semitendinosus, or semimembranosus. Groin pulls cause inner-thigh pain reproduced by resisted hip adduction. The two share mechanism and rehabilitation principles but the location and provocative tests differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Inguinal Hernia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Inguinal hernia produces a bulge in the groin that worsens with coughing or straining, often without a clear muscle injury. The adductor squeeze test is painless; the cough impulse and a palpable inguinal bulge are positive. Ultrasound with Valsalva confirms the hernia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iliotibial Band Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ITB syndrome causes lateral thigh and knee pain with running, not inner-thigh pain. Tenderness is over the lateral femoral condyle and Ober's test is positive. Groin pulls have medial-thigh tenderness and a positive adductor squeeze test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hip Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hip osteoarthritis produces deep groin and buttock pain that worsens with weight-bearing and rotation rather than adduction. Range of motion is restricted, the FADIR test is often positive, and radiographs show joint-space narrowing. Adductor strain has full passive hip range and a normal hip joint on imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares sprinting and cutting mechanisms with adductor strain; both injuries cluster in the same athletes and respond to similar progressive loading principles.

relatedConditions.name: Achilles Tendinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another lower-limb tendon overuse injury that responds to eccentric and heavy slow resistance training, the same rehabilitation framework used for chronic adductor tendinopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Shin Splints
relatedConditions.relevance: Common cumulative-load running injury that overlaps in the same athletic cohorts; both are managed with load modification, strengthening, and gradual return to sport.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Insertional enthesopathy that shares chronic tendon pathology principles, eccentric loading rehabilitation, and shockwave therapy options with chronic adductor tendinopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Runner's Knee (Patellofemoral Syndrome)
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares overuse mechanism with running athletes; hip weakness commonly underlies both, so rehabilitation often emphasizes gluteal and adductor strengthening.

faqs.question: What is a groin pull?
faqs.answer: A groin pull is a strain or partial tear of one of the inner-thigh adductor muscles, most often adductor longus, near its origin on the pubic bone. It typically happens with a sudden change of direction, kick, or slip and causes immediate inner-thigh pain reproduced when you squeeze your knees together against resistance.

faqs.question: How long does a groin pull take to heal?
faqs.answer: Grade 1 (mild) strains heal in 1-2 weeks. Grade 2 (moderate) strains take 3-6 weeks. Grade 3 (complete tear) strains need 8-12 weeks or longer with structured rehabilitation. Return to sport is decided by pain-free squeeze testing at over 90% of the uninjured side and successful sport-specific drills, not by time alone.

faqs.question: Should I keep playing through a groin pull?
faqs.answer: No. Continuing to play turns a grade 1 strain into a grade 2 or 3 tear and dramatically increases recurrence. The fastest path back to sport is to stop the activity that caused the injury, follow the first 72 hours of POLICE (protection, optimal loading, ice, compression, elevation), and start structured rehabilitation by day 3-5.

faqs.question: What is the best exercise for a groin pull?
faqs.answer: The Copenhagen adduction exercise — a side plank with the upper leg supported and the inner thigh squeezing up against the support — is the cornerstone for rehabilitation and prevention. Randomized trials show roughly a 41% reduction in adductor injuries when used twice weekly. Build progressively from short-lever to long-lever versions over 6-12 weeks.

faqs.question: How can I tell a groin pull from a hernia?
faqs.answer: A groin pull is reproduced by resisted hip adduction (squeezing the knees together) and tender over the inner-thigh muscle or pubic insertion. A hernia produces a bulge in the groin that worsens with coughing or straining, without muscle pain on adduction. Ultrasound with Valsalva imaging clarifies the diagnosis when both are possible.

faqs.question: Do I need an MRI for a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Not for most cases. Grade 1 strains are diagnosed clinically. Ultrasound is first-line if imaging is needed and shows partial tears, hematoma, and avulsion. MRI is reserved for grade 3 complete tears, symptoms persisting beyond 4-6 weeks, suspected avulsion fracture, or chronic adductor tendinopathy in athletes considering injection or surgery.

faqs.question: Can I ice a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially during the first 48-72 hours. Apply ice or a cold pack wrapped in a thin towel to the inner thigh for 10-15 minutes every 2-3 hours. Avoid direct skin contact with ice. After the first 72 hours the benefit of icing decreases and gentle progressive loading becomes more important.

faqs.question: Do groin pulls cause bruising?
faqs.answer: Grade 1 strains rarely cause visible bruising. Grade 2 strains often show bruising along the inner thigh 24-72 hours after injury as blood tracks down through fascial planes. Grade 3 tears produce significant bruising and sometimes a palpable defect or hematoma in the muscle belly.

faqs.question: Can groin pulls recur?
faqs.answer: Yes. A prior adductor strain roughly doubles the risk of recurrence in the following season. The main causes are shortened rehabilitation, residual strength deficits, and returning to sport before pain-free squeeze testing reaches over 90% of the uninjured side. Completing full Copenhagen adduction progression dramatically reduces recurrence.

faqs.question: Should I stretch a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Gentle pain-free range-of-motion exercises start in the first week. Aggressive static stretching of an inflamed muscle in the first 7-10 days can worsen the injury. Dynamic warm-up stretches (leg swings, hip openers) resume as pain settles and become part of the regular routine once you return to sport.

faqs.question: Is surgery ever needed for a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Surgery is reserved for complete proximal avulsions in high-demand athletes or chronic adductor-related groin pain that has failed 6+ months of structured rehabilitation. Adductor tenotomy and avulsion repair return 70-90% of selected athletes to their previous level. Most groin pulls (over 90%) recover fully without surgery.

faqs.question: Can women get groin pulls?
faqs.answer: Yes, though men are injured 4-5x more often in cutting sports because of higher kicking and sprinting volumes. Female athletes in football, hockey, rugby, and Australian rules football are increasingly affected as participation grows. Diagnosis and rehabilitation are identical between sexes.

faqs.question: What sports cause the most groin pulls?
faqs.answer: Football (soccer), ice hockey, rugby, Australian rules football, basketball, racquet sports, and sprint athletics lead the list. Cutting, kicking, and sliding mechanisms are the common factor. Ice hockey goaltenders are especially prone to chronic adductor tendinopathy because of the butterfly-stance demands.

faqs.question: Do I need crutches for a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Crutches are used for the first 2-7 days when walking is severely antalgic, which is most common in grade 2 and 3 injuries. Most grade 1 strains do not require crutches. Early gentle weight-bearing within pain limits is preferred over prolonged non-weight-bearing, which weakens the muscle further.

faqs.question: When can I run again after a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Return to straight-line running typically begins once you can walk briskly without pain, complete pain-free isometric and concentric adduction strengthening, and tolerate pool running or stationary cycling without discomfort. For grade 1 strains this is day 7-10; for grade 2, week 2-4; for grade 3, week 6-8.

faqs.question: Can NSAIDs slow muscle healing in a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Long-term high-dose NSAIDs may theoretically impair muscle satellite cell repair in animal studies. Short courses (3-5 days) for pain relief have not been shown to worsen outcomes in clinical trials. Paracetamol and topical NSAIDs are reasonable first-line alternatives to minimize systemic exposure.

faqs.question: What is a sports hernia and is it the same as a groin pull?
faqs.answer: Sports hernia (also called athletic pubalgia or inguinal-related groin pain) refers to a complex of inguinal canal posterior wall weakness, conjoint tendon, and rectus abdominis injuries. It can coexist with adductor strain. Pain is reproduced by resisted sit-up and cough, not just adduction. Imaging and specialist evaluation distinguish the two.

faqs.question: Does cortisone injection help groin pulls?
faqs.answer: Cortisone injections are not used in acute strains. In chronic adductor tendinopathy unresponsive to 12 weeks of rehabilitation, an ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection at the enthesis can provide short-term pain relief in 60-70% of patients at 6-8 weeks. Long-term benefits are unproven and repeated injections may weaken tendon.

faqs.question: Can children get groin pulls?
faqs.answer: Children and adolescents more often sustain bony avulsion fractures at the pubic apophysis rather than soft-tissue strains because the bone is weaker than the tendon during growth. Most avulsions heal conservatively with 6-8 weeks of restricted activity. Displacement more than 2 cm may need surgical fixation.

faqs.question: How can I prevent groin pulls?
faqs.answer: Twice-weekly Copenhagen adduction exercise during pre-season and once-weekly in-season cuts injury risk by about 41%. Maintain adductor strength at over 80% of abductor strength. Warm up dynamically for 10 minutes before high-intensity work. Build training load gradually with no more than a 10% weekly increase. The FIFA 11+ programme covers all of these elements.

faqs.question: Will a groin pull lead to long-term hip problems?
faqs.answer: A single well-rehabilitated grade 1 or 2 strain has no demonstrated long-term consequences. Recurrent strains, chronic adductor tendinopathy, and untreated coexisting femoroacetabular impingement raise the risk of long-term groin pain and modestly raise the risk of early hip osteoarthritis. Complete rehabilitation and address coexisting hip pathology to minimize that risk.

metaTitle: Groin Pull: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Groin pull (adductor strain): inner-thigh injury from cutting or kicking. Grading, recovery time, Copenhagen rehab and return-to-sport criteria, reviewed.
